questions.question:
If ' -> ' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [smile -> giggle -> laugh] is analogous to [disapprove -> _________ -> chide].

Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. reprove  B. praise  C. reprise  D. grieve
questions.to_find: The word that fits between 'disapprove' and 'chide' in increasing intensity

understand.plain: The series smile -> giggle -> laugh shows words of the same type (expressions of amusement) in increasing intensity. Similarly, disapprove -> _____ -> chide are all words expressing criticism or disapproval in increasing intensity. We need a word stronger than 'disapprove' but milder than 'chide' (to scold sharply). 'Reprove' means to rebuke gently, which fits perfectly between mild disapproval and sharp chiding.

keywords.explain: A comparison showing that two pairs of words share the same relationship
keywords.example: smile:giggle::disapprove:reprove — both pairs increase in intensity

keywords.explain: To scold or rebuke someone sharply for their behavior
keywords.example: A teacher may chide a student who repeatedly disrupts the class

keywords.explain: To rebuke or express disapproval gently but directly; stronger than disapprove but milder than chide
keywords.example: A parent might reprove a child for not finishing homework, without harsh scolding

understand.visual_alt: Intensity ladder from disapprove to reprove to chide mirroring smile to giggle to laugh

given.aim: Identify the word that sits between 'disapprove' and 'chide' in increasing intensity of criticism

terms.meaning: To have or express an unfavorable opinion; the mildest level of criticism
terms.example: She disapproved of his casual attitude
terms.connects: Starting point of the criticism spectrum, analogous to 'smile'

terms.meaning: To scold or rebuke firmly; a stronger criticism
terms.example: The coach chided the player for the costly error
terms.connects: End point of the criticism spectrum, analogous to 'laugh'

terms.meaning: To express gentle but direct disapproval; a rebuke that is firmer than disapproval but softer than chiding
terms.example: The teacher reproved the student for talking in class

given.plan: Map the intensity structure of the given analogy (smile < giggle < laugh) onto the blank series (disapprove < ? < chide) and select the option that occupies the middle intensity slot.

steps.title: Identify the intensity pattern
steps.apply: All three words express amusement; they increase in intensity: a smile is subtle, a giggle is moderate, a laugh is full-bodied.
steps.note: The pattern is: same semantic category, increasing magnitude.

steps.title: Apply the pattern to the criticism series
steps.apply: Disapprove = mild; chide = strong. The blank must be a moderate criticism word. 'Reprove' means to rebuke gently — it is firmer than merely disapproving but softer than chiding.
steps.note: 'Praise' (B) means to commend, which is opposite in direction. 'Reprise' (C) means repetition in music. 'Grieve' (D) expresses sorrow, not criticism. 'Reprove' (A) is the only match.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.apply: This mirrors smile -> giggle -> laugh perfectly: same semantic field (criticism), increasing intensity, middle word fits between the two given extremes.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas needed; this question tests semantic analogy and vocabulary.

questions.question:
In the given text, the blanks are numbered (i)-(iv). Select the best match for all the blanks.

Prof. P (i) merely a man who narrated funny stories. (ii) in his blackest moments he was capable of self-deprecating humor.
Prof. Q (iii) a man who hardly narrated funny stories. (iv) in his blackest moments was he able to find humor.

A. (i) was (ii) Even (iii) was (iv) Only
B. (i) wasn't (ii) Even (iii) was (iv) Only
C. (i) was (ii) Even (iii) wasn't (iv) Only
D. (i) was (ii) Even (iii) was (iv) Even
questions.to_find: The correct set of four words to fill blanks (i)-(iv) in the passage about Prof. P and Prof. Q

understand.plain: The passage contrasts two professors. Prof. P has a positive quality: he narrated funny stories AND could do self-deprecating humor even at his worst. Prof. Q is the opposite: he hardly narrated funny stories and could find humor only at his worst moments. We need words that maintain logical consistency within each professor's description and create a meaningful contrast between the two.

keywords.explain: Making fun of oneself in a humble or witty way
keywords.example: He told a self-deprecating joke about forgetting his own lecture notes

keywords.explain: A text pattern where two subjects are described as opposites to highlight their differences
keywords.example: Prof. P was cheerful; Prof. Q was somber — the contrast makes each description clearer

keywords.explain: Words like 'even' and 'only' that scale or restrict the meaning of what follows
keywords.example: 'Even in failure he smiled' suggests smiling in failure is surprising; 'Only in failure did he smile' restricts smiling to failure alone

understand.visual_alt: Two-column contrast: Prof. P positive humor traits vs Prof. Q limited humor traits

given.aim: Fill four blanks so that Prof. P is described as a good humorist and Prof. Q as a poor one, with logical consistency

terms.meaning: Verb for Prof. P's relation to narrating funny stories
terms.example: Prof. P ___ merely a man who narrated funny stories — 'was' fits because it sets up a positive but understated claim
terms.connects: Establishes Prof. P as someone who did narrate funny stories

terms.meaning: Adverb before 'in his blackest moments' for Prof. P
terms.example: 'Even' in his blackest moments — meaning Prof. P could do humor even when things were worst
terms.connects: Intensifies Prof. P's positive humor trait

terms.meaning: Verb for Prof. Q's relation to narrating funny stories
terms.example: Prof. Q ___ a man who hardly narrated — 'wasn't' creates the contrast with Prof. P
terms.connects: Negates Prof. Q's humor, opposite of Prof. P

terms.meaning: Adverb before 'in his blackest moments' for Prof. Q
terms.example: 'Only' in his blackest moments was he able to find humor — restricts humor to extreme moments
terms.connects: Limits Prof. Q's humor to rare worst moments, contrasting with Prof. P's 'even'

given.plan: Use the contrast logic: Prof. P 'was' (positive) + 'Even' (surprising positive extension); Prof. Q 'wasn't' (negative) + 'Only' (restricted positive).

steps.title: Determine (i): Prof. P's relationship to funny stories
steps.formula_raw: Prof. P (i) merely a man who narrated funny stories
steps.apply: The word 'merely' suggests understatement — Prof. P was not just a funny-story teller but more. So (i) = 'was' makes sense: 'P was merely a man who narrated...' sets up that he was at least that, and even more.
steps.note: 'Wasn't' would say P didn't narrate funny stories, contradicting 'merely' as understatement.

steps.title: Determine (ii): Adverb for Prof. P's blackest moments
steps.formula_raw: (ii) in his blackest moments he was capable of self-deprecating humor
steps.apply: 'Even in his blackest moments he was capable' — 'Even' is correct because it expresses that humor was present in the most unlikely (darkest) moments.
steps.note: 'Only' would restrict humor to black moments, making P seem grim — inconsistent with his positive portrait.

steps.title: Determine (iii): Prof. Q's relationship to funny stories
steps.formula_raw: Prof. Q (iii) a man who hardly narrated funny stories
steps.apply: To contrast with Prof. P ('was'), Prof. Q should be 'wasn't': 'Q wasn't a man who hardly narrated funny stories' would be double-negative. Actually 'Q wasn't merely... funny stories' creates direct contrast: Q didn't even reach the baseline P set. Hence (iii) = 'wasn't'.
steps.note: This creates clean opposition: P was (that kind of man) vs Q wasn't.

steps.title: Determine (iv): Adverb for Prof. Q's blackest moments
steps.formula_raw: (iv) in his blackest moments was he able to find humor
steps.apply: 'Only in his blackest moments was he able to find humor' — the inverted syntax 'was he able' signals a restrictive condition. 'Only' is correct: humor came to Q only at the darkest times, opposite of P's 'even' (humor present even at darkest).
steps.note: Options A and D fail because they pair Q with 'was' (should be 'wasn't'). Option B pairs P with 'wasn't' which is wrong.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas needed; this question tests logical consistency in fill-in-the-blank verbal reasoning.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

The committee wrote a _________ report, extolling only the strengths of the proposal.

A. reasonable  B. supportive  C. biased  D. fragmented
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word to describe a report that extols only the strengths of a proposal

understand.plain: The sentence tells us the committee wrote a report that extolled (praised highly) ONLY the strengths of the proposal. This means the report was one-sided — it presented only the positive side without acknowledging any weaknesses or drawbacks. A report that focuses on only one side of an argument is described as 'biased'.

keywords.example: The review extolled the film's stunning visuals while ignoring its weak storyline

keywords.explain: Unfairly prejudiced in favor of or against something; not impartial
keywords.example: A biased report presents only one side of an issue, ignoring contradictory evidence

keywords.explain: Fair, sensible, and based on good judgment
keywords.example: A reasonable report considers both pros and cons before drawing conclusions

understand.visual_alt: A weighing scale tilted to one side representing a biased, one-sided report

given.aim: Identify the adjective that best characterizes a report that only praises the strengths of a proposal

terms.meaning: The report exclusively highlights positive aspects, ignoring weaknesses
terms.example: A fair report would discuss both strengths and weaknesses; extolling only strengths is one-sided
terms.connects: This is the key clue — 'only' signals imbalance, pointing to 'biased'

terms.meaning: Leaning unfairly to one side; lacking objectivity
terms.example: A biased committee report would recommend approval regardless of flaws
terms.connects: Directly matches the description of praising only strengths

terms.example: A supportive colleague helps you through difficulties
terms.connects: A distractor — 'supportive' describes a person's attitude, not a report's objectivity

given.plan: Find the word that means 'one-sided or prejudiced,' matching the clue 'extolling only the strengths.'

steps.title: Extract the key clue
steps.apply: The word 'only' is critical. A thorough, objective report would discuss both strengths and weaknesses. By extolling ONLY the strengths, the committee wrote an unbalanced, one-sided report.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.formula_raw: A. reasonable | B. supportive | C. biased | D. fragmented
steps.apply: A. 'Reasonable' implies balance and fairness — contradicts 'only strengths'. B. 'Supportive' describes an attitude, not a report's objectivity. C. 'Biased' means prejudiced/one-sided — matches perfectly. D. 'Fragmented' means broken into pieces — unrelated to the context.
steps.note: C is the only word that captures the one-sided nature described.

questions.formula_note: No formulas required; this is a vocabulary and contextual reasoning question.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

If the country has to achieve real prosperity, it is _________ that the fruits of progress reach all, and in equal measure.

A. inevitable  B. contingent  C. oblivious  D. imperative
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word expressing that equal distribution of prosperity is essential or necessary

understand.plain: The sentence argues that for a country to achieve real prosperity, the fruits of progress must reach everyone equally. The blank needs a word that conveys strong necessity — something that is not merely desirable but absolutely required. 'Imperative' means absolutely necessary or urgent, making it the best fit.

keywords.explain: Of vital importance; absolutely necessary; an obligation or urgent requirement
keywords.example: It is imperative that safety protocols be followed in a laboratory

keywords.explain: Certain to happen; unable to be avoided
keywords.example: With proper policies, equitable growth is not inevitable — it must be planned

keywords.explain: Dependent on certain conditions; not certain
keywords.example: The project's success is contingent on securing funding

understand.visual_alt: A country's prosperity represented as a pie chart that must be shared equally among all citizens

given.aim: Find the word that conveys the idea that equal distribution of progress is absolutely necessary for real prosperity

terms.meaning: Genuine, widespread well-being — not just aggregate economic growth
terms.example: A country where only the top 1% is wealthy has not achieved real prosperity
terms.connects: The subject that requires something specific for it to be achieved

terms.meaning: Benefits of development must be distributed to every citizen, not just a few
terms.example: Infrastructure, education, and healthcare reaching rural areas as well as cities
terms.connects: This condition is described as [blank] — i.e., it must be so

terms.meaning: An absolute necessity; something that must be done without compromise
terms.example: Equal access to justice is imperative in a democratic society
terms.connects: Correctly fills the blank — the equal distribution is not optional but mandatory

given.plan: The blank must express strong necessity. Evaluate: inevitable (certainty of occurrence), contingent (conditionality), oblivious (unawareness), imperative (absolute necessity). The context calls for necessity, not inevitability.

steps.title: Understand the logical requirement
steps.apply: The sentence uses a conditional structure: 'If the country has to achieve real prosperity, it is ___ that fruits reach all.' The blank is a predicate adjective — it must characterize the requirement for equal distribution.
steps.note: The word must convey 'this is a must' rather than 'this will happen automatically.'

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.formula_raw: A. inevitable | B. contingent | C. oblivious | D. imperative
steps.apply: A. 'Inevitable' means it will happen anyway — removes the sense of obligation. B. 'Contingent' means dependent on conditions — weakens the claim. C. 'Oblivious' means unaware — completely wrong in context. D. 'Imperative' means absolutely necessary — perfectly conveys that equal distribution is a non-negotiable requirement.
steps.note: The sentence calls for moral/logical necessity, which 'imperative' captures exactly.

questions.formula_note: No formulas required; this is a vocabulary and contextual reasoning question.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence:

The two child norm with _________ for the violators will have significant implications for our demographic profile.

A. disincentives  B. incitements  C. restrictions  D. restraints
questions.to_find: The most appropriate word for measures aimed at discouraging violation of the two child norm

understand.plain: The sentence refers to a policy called the 'two child norm' — a guideline limiting families to two children. People who violate this norm (have more than two children) face certain consequences. The blank describes what those consequences are called. Since these measures are meant to discourage violation (not physically restrict or incite), the correct word is 'disincentives' — measures that reduce the attractiveness of a behavior.

keywords.explain: A population policy that encourages families to limit themselves to two children
keywords.example: Many countries have implemented two child norms to control population growth

keywords.explain: Measures that discourage a particular action by making it less attractive or more costly
keywords.example: Loss of government job benefits for having more than two children is a disincentive

keywords.explain: Physical or legal controls that prevent an action from happening
keywords.example: A speed limiter on a car is a mechanical restraint on driving speed

understand.visual_alt: Population growth chart showing the demographic effect of a two child norm with disincentives for violators

given.aim: Identify the word that describes consequence-based discouragement measures for those who violate the two child norm

terms.meaning: An expected standard of behavior; in policy, a guideline (not a hard law)
terms.example: A social norm is followed voluntarily; violations bring social or policy consequences
terms.connects: Because it is a 'norm' and not a law, violations are discouraged rather than physically prevented

terms.meaning: Those who break or do not follow the norm
terms.example: A family with three children would be considered a violator of the two child norm
terms.connects: The measures (blank) apply to these people to discourage others from similar behavior

terms.meaning: Policies or penalties that make a behavior less appealing or more costly without outright banning it
terms.example: Denial of promotions or tax benefits to those with more than two children
terms.connects: Correctly fills the blank: disincentives discourage violation without physically restraining

given.plan: A norm (not a law) uses disincentives (not restrictions or restraints) to discourage behavior. Incitements would encourage violation — opposite meaning. Restraints imply physical/legal barriers. Disincentives are policy-based discouragements appropriate for a 'norm.'

steps.title: Understand what follows a 'norm' vs a 'law'
steps.apply: A norm is a guideline, not an enforceable law. When people violate a norm, the response is typically to make violation less attractive (disincentives) rather than to physically prevent it (restraints/restrictions).
steps.note: This narrows the choice to 'disincentives' over 'restrictions' or 'restraints.'

steps.title: Eliminate each option
steps.formula_raw: A. disincentives | B. incitements | C. restrictions | D. restraints
steps.apply: B. 'Incitements' means encouragements to act — opposite of what is needed. C. 'Restrictions' implies banned behavior — too strong for a norm. D. 'Restraints' implies physical prevention — again too strong. A. 'Disincentives' means penalties/consequences that discourage without prohibiting — perfect for a policy norm.
steps.note: The word 'disincentives' is standard policy language for measures used with population norms.

questions.formula_note: No formulas required; this is a vocabulary and contextual reasoning question about policy terminology.
